title: Self-Assessment
content:
Throughout the 12-week First Cloud AI Journey program, I had the opportunity to learn and practice AWS services, progressing from fundamental concepts to real-world infrastructure deployment. At the same time, I participated in building the ZeroBug Agent workshop in the role of TV1 — IAM & VPC Foundation.
My responsibilities in the workshop included creating IAM Users and IAM Roles for team members and AWS services, deploying Amazon VPC, creating Public Subnets and Private Subnets, and configuring Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, Route Tables, and Security Groups to establish the networking and security foundation for the entire system. After completing the work, I handed over infrastructure details such as VPC ID, Subnet ID, Security Group, and IAM Role ARN to the next team members so they could continue deploying Amazon S3, Amazon RDS, Amazon EC2, AWS Lambda, Step Functions, API Gateway, CloudFront, and AWS WAF. Through this process, I strengthened my skills in AWS network architecture design, access control management with IAM, and implementing infrastructure following a Public/Private Subnet model.
In terms of work ethic, I always tried to complete my tasks according to schedule, actively studied the official AWS documentation and workshop materials whenever I encountered issues related to IAM, VPC, NAT Gateway, Route Tables, and Security Groups, and ensured that other team members could deploy their work smoothly on the same infrastructure.
To reflect objectively on my participation in the program, I would like to evaluate myself based on the following criteria:
No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 Professional Knowledge & Skills Understanding and implementing IAM Users, IAM Roles, Amazon VPC, Subnets, Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, Route Tables, and Security Groups for the ZeroBug Agent system ☐ ✅ ☐ 2 Learning Ability Quickly acquiring knowledge of AWS networking architecture, identity management, and access control through IAM ☐ ✅ ☐ 3 Proactiveness Actively researching AWS documentation and completing hands-on labs to deploy the system’s network infrastructure ✅ ☐ ☐ 4 Sense of Responsibility Completing assigned infrastructure tasks on schedule and handing them over properly to team members ✅ ☐ ☐ 5 Discipline Maintaining consistent learning, practice, and progress toward planned goals throughout the 12-week program ✅ ☐ ☐ 6 Progressive Mindset Actively exploring best practices in AWS network architecture, security, and infrastructure deployment ☐ ✅ ☐ 7 Communication Exchanging information, reporting progress, and guiding team members in using the deployed infrastructure ✅ ☐ ☐ 8 Teamwork Collaborating with team members during deployment and handing over shared infrastructure details ✅ ☐ ☐ 9 Professional Conduct Always showing respect, support, and effective cooperation with team members ✅ ☐ ☐ 10 Problem-Solving Skills Addressing issues related to IAM configuration, VPC, Route Tables, NAT Gateway, and Security Groups during deployment ☐ ✅ ☐ 11 Contribution to the Project Successfully deploying the IAM and VPC foundation to support the ZeroBug Agent system ☐ ✅ ☐ 12 Overall Overall evaluation of my participation in the First Cloud AI Journey program ☐ ✅ ☐ Areas for Improvement Continue improving my knowledge of AWS network architecture design and cloud security. Strengthen my ability to optimize and deploy infrastructure for large-scale real-world systems. Expand my experience in applying AWS services to future projects and software systems.

title: Security Groups
content:
Security groups → Create security group (VPC = zerobug-vpc).
Order: ALB-SG → EC2-SG → Lambda-SG → RDS-SG.
zerobug-alb-sg Inbound Port Source HTTP 80 0.0.0.0/0 HTTPS (optional) 443 0.0.0.0/0 Outbound: default All traffic.
zerobug-ec2-sg Inbound Port Source Custom TCP 8080 zerobug-alb-sg (select SG, not IP) SSH (optional) 22 My IP Outbound All traffic All 0.0.0.0/0 If Outbound rules = 0: EC2 cannot call S3, Secrets, RDS, or the internet. Toàn will hit errors during deploy. Always add All traffic → 0.0.0.0/0.
zerobug-lambda-sg Inbound: not required. Outbound: All traffic → 0.0.0.0/0 (HTTPS to Bedrock Mantle us-east-1). zerobug-rds-sg Inbound Port Source PostgreSQL 5432 zerobug-ec2-sg PostgreSQL 5432 zerobug-lambda-sg Copy 4 Security Group IDs into the parameter table.

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
Overall Assessment 1. Program Content
The program is structured in a reasonable progression, starting with fundamental AWS knowledge before moving on to practical implementation. The combination of theory and hands-on practice gives learners the opportunity to apply what they have learned immediately. As a result, when they entered the team Workshop, the implementation of the assigned infrastructure tasks became much smoother.
2. Quality of Materials
The learning materials and Workshop instructions are presented in a fairly detailed manner, with illustrations and clear step-by-step guidance. Dividing the content by team members helps define the scope of work precisely and reduces overlap during implementation. In addition, AWS official documentation is also a valuable reference source for understanding each service more deeply.
3. Practical Value of the Program
One of the program’s strengths is that it does not only introduce AWS services individually, but also teaches how to combine them into a complete system. In the ZeroBug Agent Workshop, the implementation of IAM and VPC helped establish a shared foundation for the whole system and clarified the connections between services such as EC2, Lambda, API Gateway, CloudFront, and WAF.
4. Skills Gained
After 12 weeks of study and practice, knowledge of identity management with IAM, network infrastructure design using Amazon VPC, Public/Private Subnet, NAT Gateway, and Security Groups was significantly strengthened. At the same time, planning, teamwork, and infrastructure handover skills among team members also improved through the Workshop process.
5. Challenges and Lessons Learned
Biggest challenge: Designing the network architecture and IAM permissions so that team members can share the infrastructure while still ensuring security and manageability. Another challenge: Synchronizing infrastructure parameters and handing them over accurately to the next team members to avoid affecting the implementation timeline. Lesson learned: Resource naming conventions should be standardized from the beginning, a clear implementation plan should be established, and communication between team members should be maintained regularly to reduce mistakes during execution. 6. Program Support
The AWS Credits provided by the program made it much easier to practice in a real AWS environment without worrying too much about cost. In addition, the Workshop sessions and instructional materials were highly supportive throughout the learning and project implementation process, and I received a great deal of support from the program admins and others.
Additional Questions What was the most satisfying part of participating in the program?
→ The program admins were very dedicated in supporting me, giving thoughtful advice and clear explanations that were easy to understand. I was also able to directly implement AWS infrastructure for a complete system and work with team members to build the product through a real-world workflow.
What should the program improve?
→ It should add more in-depth practical sessions on AWS architecture optimization, common troubleshooting, and sharing experiences from real projects.
Would you be willing to recommend the program to others? Why?
→ Yes. The program is suitable for students who want to learn AWS in a structured way, combining theory and practice while also gaining experience through team projects and events.
Suggestions and Expectations Add more Workshops on cost optimization, system monitoring, and security on the AWS platform. Increase the number of scenario-based labs so learners have more opportunities to practice. Organize more sharing sessions with mentors or experienced professionals who have implemented AWS projects in companies. Continue maintaining the learning model that combines group Workshops to improve both technical knowledge and teamwork skills.

title: Monitoring & Operations
content:
Shared section — apply during deployment (5.3–5.7) and when debugging section 5.8. Each member monitors their own resources; you do not need to wait for E2E tests before enabling basic logs/alarms.
Monitoring by block Block Member CloudWatch / tools Notes VPC / NAT Trí VPC Reachability Analyzer; NAT status Mantle Lambda timeout → check NAT + route S3 / RDS / Secrets Kiệt RDS metrics; Secrets get-value test pgvector: \d code_embeddings on psql EC2 / ALB Toàn Target Group health; journalctl -u zerobug Unhealthy usually means JAR not started Lambda / Step Fn Hoa /aws/lambda/*; Step Fn execution history Log embed + chat Mantle; HTTP 401 → API key Edge / Auth Trinh API GW access logs; WAF sampled requests 401 JWT vs 502 backend Bedrock Mantle & cost Chat inference (openai.gpt-oss-120b) and embedding (cohere.embed-multilingual-v3) appear on AWS Billing / Cost Explorer — same bill as NAT, EC2, RDS. Mantle API key TTL ~30 days — env BEDROCK_MANTLE_API_KEY on ContextBuilderLambda and BedrockInvokeLambda; rotate before expiry (5.9.2). NAT Gateway ~32 USD/month — highest cost after running the workshop; delete early when not in use (5.10). Suggested alarms & budget (workshop) Alarm Suggested threshold Who handles ALB UnHealthyHostCount ≥ 1 for 5 minutes Toàn Lambda Errors ≥ 1 Hoa RDS CPUUtilization > 80% (db.t3.micro) Kiệt AWS Budgets Email when team threshold exceeded Entire team Frontend (when app is running): GET /api/aws/status — RDS, S3, Bedrock Mantle status.
Content CloudWatch Logs & Alarms AWS Budgets & SNS Debug by member Section 5.9 does not require all alarms before demo — prioritize logs + Step Fn history when debugging. Budgets/SNS should be enabled early to avoid leaving NAT/WAF running overnight.

title: Resource Cleanup
content:
Required after workshop — delete resources in reverse deployment order to avoid dependency errors and save costs (NAT, WAF, RDS, EC2 billed hourly).
Deletion order (entire team) Trinh (CloudFront, WAF, API GW, Cognito) → Hoa (Step Functions → 6 Lambda → log groups) → Toàn (ALB, Target Group, EC2) → Kiệt (RDS → subnet group → Secrets → S3 empty + delete bucket) → Trí (NAT → EIP → IGW → SG → subnet → VPC → IAM roles) → Shared: IAM users/group, Budgets, revoke Bedrock API key Step Member Main resources Notes 1 Trinh CloudFront, WAF, API Gateway, Cognito WAF Global — wait for disassociate 2 Hoa State machine, 6 Lambda PascalCase Delete Step Fn before Lambda 3 Toàn ALB, Target Group, EC2 Terminate EC2 before delete ALB 4 Kiệt RDS, DB subnet group, Secret, S3 S3 must be empty before delete bucket 5 Trí NAT, EIP, IGW, 4 SG, VPC NAT + EIP costly — delete as early as possible 6 Shared IAM group/users/keys, Budgets, Bedrock key Revoke one API key shared by 2 Mantle Lambdas Pre-completion checklist No EC2 running, RDS Available, NAT Available ZeroBug S3 bucket empty and deleted No ZeroBug Lambda / Step Functions remaining CloudFront distribution Disabled then deleted Workshop Bedrock API key revoked (us-east-1) Current month billing: no NAT, EIP, RDS, EC2 line items (may prorate a few hours) Content Trinh — Edge & Auth Hoa — Serverless Toàn — EC2 & ALB Kiệt — Data Trí — VPC & NAT IAM & billing confirmation NAT Gateway + Elastic IP and WAF are the most expensive — if pausing overnight, still delete NAT or terminate EC2/RDS. Do not leave NAT running through the week after the workshop.
Lambda cleanup names match the parameter table: ProjectImportLambda, SourceFileServiceLambda, ContextBuilderLambda, BedrockInvokeLambda, ResultServiceLambda, HistoryServiceLambda.
